coredata.dc:title: Synthetic peptide (DP1) functionalized graphene oxide: A biocompatible nanoformulation with broad-spectrum antibacterial and antibiofilm activity
coredata.dc:creator: Joshi, Shubhi
Chadha, Jatin
Harjai, Kusum
Verma, Gaurav
Saini, Avneet
coredata.dc:description: In the present scenario, clinical success of all existing antimicrobial regimes has been severely challenged by bacterial infections and emergence of multidrug-resistant strains necessitating the development of improved strategies with broad-spectrum bactericidal effects. With this objective, a multifunctional nanoformulation was synthesized by covalently coupling a novel synthetic antimicrobial peptide DP1 (RFGRFLRKILRFLKK) to graphene oxide (GO). This bioconjugated two-dimensional sheet (GO-DP1) with nanosized dimensions (32 nm) proficiently inhibited the growth of Gram-positive (Bacillus subtilis, Staphylococcus aureus) as well as Gram-negative (Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhi) bacteria along with multidrug-resistant bacterial strains namely Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was 2 to 8 folds lower as compared to GO and in the effective range of peptide’s MIC. The conjugation between biotic (peptide) and abiotic (GO) surface combined the advantageous properties of these two classes of materials thereby signifying synergy. The biomedical applicability of GO-DP1 is also successfully demonstrated by evaluating its hemocompatibility and antibiofilm potency in a Pseudomonas aeruginosa contaminated hypodermic needle model. This is the first study of its kind that paves the way for future applications of this nanoformulation in environments and settings that serve as reservoirs of microbial contamination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation displaying chemical conjugation protocol for synthesis of peptide-functionalized graphene oxide nanoformulation (a-b). Herein GO synthesized by modified Hummers method was covalently conjugated with peptide DP1 by application of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride and N-Hydroxysuccinimide as cross-linkers between the two materials.

Characterization results of the synthesized GO-DP1 nanoformulation. XRD spectra of GO-DP1 in comparison with GO (a). Comparative FT-IR spectra (b), elemental mapping and elemental weight percentage (%) (c-d) of graphite, GO, DP1 and GO-DP1.

Comparative HR-TEM micrographs of graphite, GO and GO-DP1 (a). AFM micrographs (b) and 3D topology of graphite (i-ii), GO (iii-iv) and GO-DP1 (v-vi). The zeta potential values (c) and water contact angle measurements (d) of tested specimen. The error bars represent mean ± SD (standard deviation) from three independent experiments.

Minimum concentrations of DP1, GO and GO-DP1 required for inhibition of different bacterial strains (a). *denotes the fold change in MIC with respect to GO. Pictorial representation displaying diameter of inhibition zones (in mm) formed on agar plates by DP1, GO and GO-DP1 against different Gram-negative, Gram-positive and multidrug-resistant organisms after incubating samples in wells for 24 h at 37 °C (b).
Model depicting anticipated antibacterial action of peptide-functionalized nanoformulation.
In vitro hemolytic activity of GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 towards erythrocytes (a-c) and cell viability towards 3T3-L1 (d) at different concentrations. Values represent mean ± SD (n = 6).

(a) Quantitative analysis of P. aeruginosa biofilm inhibition pattern evaluated at 600 nm in the presence of GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 at sub-minimum inhibitory concentration, (b) Displays qualitative biofilm inhibition assay. Biofilm adherence scores have been mentioned above each experimental group wherein symbols ++++, +++, ++ and + denotes very strong, strong, moderate and weak adherence, respectively. FESEM micrographs of untreated P.aeruginosa biofilm at peak day (c-d), along with effect of GO (e-f), DP1 (g-h) and GO-DP1 (i-j) on biofilm inhibition. The arrows indicate production of extracellular polymeric substances within the P. aeruginosa biofilms at 5.00 µm and 3.00 µm magnification.

Clinical application of GO-DP1. (a) Experimental setup for evaluation of antibiofilm activity in P. aeruginosa infected hypodermic needles. (b) Crystal violet stained hypodermic needles incubated with GO, DP1 and GO-DP1, respectively. (c) Antibacterial effect of GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 incubated P. aeruginosa infected hypodermic needles in terms of colony forming units. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
List of bacterial strains used in the study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present scenario, clinical success of all existing antimicrobial regimes has been severely challenged by bacterial infections and emergence of multidrug-resistant strains necessitating the development of improved strategies with broad-spectrum bactericidal effects. With this objective, a multifunctional nanoformulation was synthesized by covalently coupling a novel synthetic antimicrobial peptide DP1 (RFGRFLRKILRFLKK) to graphene oxide (GO). This bioconjugated two-dimensional sheet (GO-DP1) with nanosized dimensions (32 nm) proficiently inhibited the growth of Gram-positive (Bacillus subtilis, Staphylococcus aureus) as well as Gram-negative (Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhi) bacteria along with multidrug-resistant bacterial strains namely Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was 2 to 8 folds lower as compared to GO and in the effective range of peptide’s MIC. The conjugation between biotic (peptide) and abiotic (GO) surface combined the advantageous properties of these two classes of materials thereby signifying synergy. The biomedical applicability of GO-DP1 is also successfully demonstrated by evaluating its hemocompatibility and antibiofilm potency in a Pseudomonas aeruginosa contaminated hypodermic needle model. This is the first study of its kind that paves the way for future applications of this nanoformulation in environments and settings that serve as reservoirs of microbial contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria are transmitted through air, water, food or living vectors and hence cause diseases that can occur at any body site and can have a drastic impact causing morbidity and mortality. Also, the growth of pathogenic microorganisms as biofilms; increases the pathogens’ ability to repel and tolerate antimicrobial regimes [1]. Antibiotics since their discovery in the 1940s have revolutionized medicine and provided cures for several serious and fatal infectious diseases. Unfortunately, their widespread, uncontrolled and irrational use over the years has led to rapid development of bacterial resistance against several antibiotics. The World Health Organization (WHO) has reported antimicrobial resistance (AMR) as one of the potential public health crisis [2]. Notably, in the last few decades, the number of approved antibiotics by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) was less than that of the reported cases of antibiotic-resistant bacteria [3–7].

Healthcare-associated infections have skyrocketed in recent years due to the emergence of multidrug-resistant (MDR), extensively drug-resistant (XDR) and pandrug-resistant (PDR) bacterial isolates. Initially, the occurrence of resistant bacterial strains was restricted to hospital environments but factors such as globalization and unregulated use due to access to over-the-counter antibiotics has resulted in bacterial adaptation to conventional antibiotics. The emergence of drug-resistant bacterial organisms is anticipated as one of the most pressing challenges in the history of public health as infections caused by antibiotic-resistant bacteria are challenging to treat due to their proclivity to relapse, resulting in increased morbidity and mortality [1,2].

The current approaches to combat multidrug resistance include early detection of infection, use of strategies to avert transmission of infection and prescribing bactericidal along with sterilizing drugs [8–10]. These conventional therapies have limited efficacy, increased incidences of hepatotoxicity and prolonged duration of treatment. This often results in non-compliance of the patient towards prescribed course of treatment [5,6]. Further, repeated antibiotic treatments result in increased tolerance in the surviving bacterial cells which proves to be even more fatal. The COVID-19 pandemic has further fuelled this fire since patients have been exposed to repeated therapies consisting of antibiotics, exacerbating the future problem of multidrug resistance [11].

Thus, numerous novel approaches are being explored to curb such infections, control pathogenic organisms and ensure public health safety. Amongst the various antimicrobial agents, antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) are being extensively explored as an alternative to the conventional use of antibiotics owing to their broad-spectrum antibacterial and immunomodulatory activity [12–17]. These small-sized, cationic molecules are of great interest particularly due to the low propensity of bacteria to develop resistance towards them [18,19]. However, their applicability is restricted by chemical and proteolytic degradation along with hemolytic toxicity [20,21]. These limitations can be handled by conjugating the peptides with non-biological nanoparticles (NPs) thereby leading to the formation of functional scaffolds [22,23]. Amongst the different organic and inorganic nanoparticles, graphitic derivative graphene oxide (GO) displayed promising properties for peptide conjugation as the occurrence of high-density oxygen moieties such as epoxide, hydroxyl and carboxyl may facilitate biomolecule immobilization through the formation of amide linkages [24,25]. Also, GO’s compatibility within the biological system makes it a promising binding material for the synthesis of peptide-functionalized nanoconjugates [26–31]. We anticipate that conjugation of AMPs with GO may in turn facilitate the increase in peptide concentration thereby enhancing the bioavailability of peptides as GO possesses a large surface area and exhibits good water dispersibility [32]. These unique physicochemical properties of GO seemed highly suitable for developing diverse hybrid materials, particularly for biomedical applications [27,33–37].

In this study, a novel cationic peptide DP1 (RFGRFLRKILRFLKK) recently designed in our laboratory was conjugated with nano-sized GO (Fig. 1 ) [13,38]. DP1 was covalently conjugated with GO to synthesize a biotic-abiotic platform that is biocompatible and at the same time demonstrates significant antibacterial activity. Successful synthesis of the nanoformulation was confirmed by conducting extensive spectroscopic (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) and microscopic (High-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Field emission scanning electron microscopy and Atomic force microscopy) characterizations. Size and zeta-potential measurements along with thermogravimetric analysis and wettability studies were instrumental in determining the charge and stability of the conjugate. Antibacterial efficacy of the nanoformulation was established by determination of minimum inhibitory concentrations along with zone of inhibition studies against standard and multidrug-resistant bacterial strains followed by antibiofilm studies. In-vitro hemocompatibility and cytotoxicity of the nanoformulation was also examined. Furthermore, biomedical applicability of the nanoformulation was evaluated in a Pseudomonas aeruginosa contaminated needle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene oxide nano powder
ce:section.ce:para: Nanosized GO was synthesized according to modified Hummers method [39,40]. Natural graphite flake (0.5 g), NaNO3 (0.5 g), and H2SO4 (23 mL) were mixed under vigorous stirring in an ice bath followed by the slow and cautious addition of KMnO4 (3 g). Then the mixture was transferred to a water bath in which temperature was maintained for 35 °C and stirred for 1 h. Distilled water (40 mL) was added and the bath temperature was increased up to 90 °C. The reaction mixture was stirred for 30 min followed by the addition of 100 mL of distilled water. Next, dropwise addition of 30 % H2O2 (3 mL) was carried out to stop the reaction. The obtained reaction mixture was centrifuged (10000g) and washed using de-ionized (DI) water to obtain a neutral pH. The resultant filter cake was dried using a hot air oven and redispersed into DI water to a final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. The final solution was sonicated for 90 min to produce fine GO powder [41,42].

Peptide was covalently conjugated with GO by a previously reported method [28,34]. In this study, the covalently conjugated GO-peptide complex is abbreviated as GO-DP1. In a typical protocol, powdered GO was dissolved in DI water followed by sonication for 30 min to obtain a clear solution of GO. At pH 5–5.5, cross-linkers, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were successively added into the GO solution and sonicated for 1 h to generate semi-stable NHS esters linkage with GO. Then, pH of the mixture was adjusted to 8. The peptides (dissolved in phosphate buffer solution (PBS), 0.145 mM, 0.1 mL) were added to the obtained mixture followed by incubation at 4 °C in dark for 12 h to enable cross-linking of the NHS esters with peptides. Unbound peptides were removed from the final solution by centrifugation at 11,750 g for 30 min. The obtained sediment was then re-dispersed in DI water and centrifuged several times to obtain a homogenous solution. The obtained solution was lyophilized for future use.

Chemical and molecular structure of the synthesized samples were investigated using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy using Perkin Elmer-Spectrum RX-IFT-IR (USA) spectrophotometer. All the samples were scanned between a range of 800 to 4000 cm−1.

X-ray diffraction patterns of the synthesized peptide-functionalized graphene oxide nanoconjugate was determined using a PANalytical X'Pert Pro XRD. Cu K-alpha-1 was used as a radiation source wherein nickel metal was used as beta filter.

The zeta potential of all the samples was measured using DelsaNano C Zeta Potential Analyzer. Measurements were recorded at 25 °C ± 1 °C using scattering angle of 90° and acquisition time of 3 min in triplicate. Data was processed and analyzed using Delsa™Nano software.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were recorded using Veeco diINNOV atomic force microscope at tapping mode. Resonance frequency of 300 kHz and force constant 40 N/m were used for scanning. Monolithic silicon probe was used to analyze the samples at room temperature. For AFM measurements, 10 µl sample was coated on one-micron sized freshly cleaved mica substrate and air-dried for 30 min followed by washing with milli-Q water. Before final analysis the prepared samples were incubated for 24 h. The data was processed using Gwyddion 2.36 software.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs were obtained using JEOL HRTEM equipment operating at 200 kV voltage. The samples were cast dropped on carbon coated grids and dried at room temperature (37 °C) for 12 h prior to analysis.

Water contact angle (WCA) measurements were carried out using KRÜSS Scientific - Drop Shape Analyzer (DSA25E). In a typical experiment, the conjugates were drop casted on a glass substrate and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C before analysis. Liquid droplets of DI water were used for conducting water contact angle measurements. The contact angle was recorded by placing DI drop three times on different sections of the conjugate surface.

The experimental protocols were approved by the Research Monitoring Committee, Panjab University and also, Institutional Biosafety Committee, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India (IBSC-PANUNI-038–2020) (See Table 1 ).

The current work involved evaluation of antibacterial activity against different Gram-negative (Salmonella typhi, Escherichia coli, multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Gram-positive strains of Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus subtilis, Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus strains provided by Department of Microbiology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. The antibiotic resistance profile of the clinical strains has been briefly documented in Table S1 (Supplementary data).

Microtiter broth dilution method was used to determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) by a previously described protocol [22]. The bacterial culture was inoculated in Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and incubated overnight at 37 °C. The following day, fresh LB broth was inoculated using the overnight culture and incubated at 37 °C to obtain cells in the mid-exponential growth phase. The bacterial culture was diluted with sterile broth to obtain 108 CFU/mL. Using a multichannel pipette, sterile broth was dispensed into the wells of a microtiter plate. The test samples containing graphite, GO, and GO-DP1 were dispensed into the wells and serially diluted (2 folds) till the desired concentration was achieved. The standardized bacterial culture was then added to each well and the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a static incubator [22]. Wells with only sterile broth and broth-containing bacterial culture (without agent) served as negative and positive controls, respectively. Post-incubation, absorbance values were recorded at 600 nm. MIC was regarded as the minimum concentration of the agent that significantly inhibited bacterial growth by more than 50 % compared to the untreated control. MICs were also confirmed using the resazurin dye reduction method. All experiments were performed in triplicates to avoid errors.

Antibacterial activity of the synthesized G, GO, and GO-DP1 was determined using the agar well diffusion assay method. Approximately 20 mL of nutrient agar was poured in sterilized Petri dishes. The plates were left overnight at room temperature to check for any contamination to appear. The bacterial test organisms were grown in nutrient broth for 24 h. A 100 mL nutrient broth culture of each bacterial organism (1 × 108 CFU/ml) was used to prepare bacterial lawns. Three agar wells of 5 mm diameter were prepared. The wells were loaded with 20 μl of the synthesized nanoparticles. The plates containing the bacterial and nanoparticles were incubated at 37 °C. The plates were examined for the presence of zones of inhibition, which appear as a clear area around the wells. The diameter of such inhibition zones was measured for each sample and expressed in millimetres [43,44].

Compatibility of the formulated conjugate was assessed by evaluating hemolytic toxicity in human erythrocytes. Fresh blood from healthy volunteer was obtained in heparin-treated tubes. Further, erythrocytes were separated from blood by mixing the collected blood with PBS and centrifuged for 10 min at 3000 rpm at 5 °C. The collected erythrocytes were washed with PBS 4 times. Triton X-100 mixed with RBCs was used as a positive control, while PBS mixed with RBCs was used as a negative control. The formulations were added to the RBC suspension and incubated for 3 h with gentle shaking. Following incubation, samples were centrifuged at 10,016 g for 3 min and the supernatants were carefully collected for measurement. Degree of hemolysis induced by the samples was determined by measuring the absorption of the supernatants at 540 nm using UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) [34].

The effect of the synthesized graphitic materials and their respective peptide-functionalized nano conjugates on the 3T3-L1 cells was evaluated by conducting MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay. The cell lines were obtained from National Centre for Cell Science, Pune, India. Cells were cultured and maintained in dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % (vol/vol) bovine serum, 100 U/ml penicillin and 100 μg/ml streptomycin, in 5 % carbon dioxide (CO2) incubator with 98 % humidity at 37 °C. Before experimentation, the cells were harvested by trypsinization and seeded in 96-well plate. Further, upon reaching 75–80 % confluency the cells were treated with the specimen at concentration of 5 μg/mL – 500 μg/mL for 24 h at 37 °C in 5 % CO2. After 24 h the cells were treated with MTT (5 mg/ml) for a period of 4 h. Then cells were lysed with molecular grade dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Absorbance was taken at 570 nm and compared with the wells containing untreated cells. The percentage of cell viability was calculated using Eq. (3).

Biofilm inhibition assay was performed in the presence of GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 at sub-MICs in 96 well flatbottom microtiter plates (Tarsons, Kolkata, India). Wells containing 5 mL LB medium supplemented with sub-MICs of the drug(s) were inoculated with a diluted culture of P. aeruginosa (1 × 106 CFU/ml). The plate was incubated at 37 °C under static conditions. Sterile LB was used as a negative control, while PAO1 culture without any drug treatment served as a positive control. The media containing planktonic cells was discarded; wells were washed thrice with PBS (pH 7.2), and the adherent biofilms were stained with 0.1 % -crystal violet. After staining, the dye was removed, and wells were washed thrice with PBS (pH 7.2) to remove excess -crystal violet. The plate was air-dried at 37 °C, followed by adding 33 % glacial acetic acid to each well to elute the bound dye. Absorbance values were measured using an ELISA plate reader (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA) at 595 nm. All sets were processed similarly for up to 7 days.

Overnight culture of PAO1 was diluted using sterile LB to attain 1 × 106 CFU/ml and 200 mL of the adjusted culture was dispensed into a 96 well flat-bottom microtiter plate to allow biofilm formation. After every 24 h, the spent culture medium was discarded, and fresh LB was replenished in the wells. Cultures were allowed to grow at 37 °C under static conditions for 1 day (young biofilm), 4 days (peak biofilm), and 7 days (mature biofilm). On completion of the designated time, preformed biofilms were challenged with sub-MICs of GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 for 24 h at 37 °C. Wells lacking the specimen served as positive control, while sterile LB was used as negative control. The contents were aspirated to remove any planktonic cells from the treated and untreated wells, followed by washing thrice with PBS. After washing, adherent biofilms were stained with 0.1 % crystal violet, followed by washing with PBS. The bound crystal violet was then eluted from adherent biofilms using 33 % acetic acid and the absorbance values were recorded at 595 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic examination of P. aeruginosa biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: FESEM was employed to examine biofilm formation in PAO1 following drug treatment visually (Bose et al., 2020). PAO1 biofilms were allowed to form over sterile coverslips placed in 5 mL of LB medium supplemented with sub-MICs of GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 in a six-well culture dish at 37 °C for 4 days. PAO1 culture without any drug specimen served as a positive control. After every 24 h, the culture medium was discarded, and fresh LB containing the specimen was added to the wells. Post incubation, the spent media was aspirated and coverslips were washed with PBS (pH 7.2) to remove any unbound cells. Peak day biofilms were fixed with 2 % glutaraldehyde for 2 h at room temperature, followed by three rounds of washing with PBS. Biofilms were then sequentially dehydrated using different concentrations of EtOH, including 30, 50, 70, 90, and 100 %. Coverslips were air-dried, gold-coated, and viewed under the Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM; SU8010, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) to analyse PAO1 biofilms.

The peptide DP1 was conjugated with nanosized GO by covalent interaction approach using EDC/NHS as linkers for the coupling process. The combination of EDC/NHS was utilised to facilitate coupling of carboxyl groups to primary amines. During this, the pH at which carbodiimide reaction takes place plays a crucial role as reactivity of carbodiimides is highly dependent on the pH of the reaction environment. Typically, the carbodiimide reaction is most effective at a slightly acidic pH like that of aqueous GO [45–47]. However, pH of the solution is increased before adding peptide in order to facilitate amination reaction [48,49]. The peptide through its N-terminal NH2 group is coupled via the use of linkers to the surface of GO. This facilitated the covalent coupling of DP1 to GO while avoiding any type of secondary structure perturbation of the peptide. This in turn is critical for the efficacy of DP1 as the peptide's secondary structure dictates its antibacterial properties as well as hemolytic behaviour [34,50].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the nanoformulation (Fig. 2 a) displayed multiple peaks at 11°, 21°, 26.8°, 29.4° and 42.6°. Whereas, XRD spectra of GO exhibited peaks at 11°, 26.7° and 42°, respectively. It was observed that the characteristic peaks of GO were present in both the conjugate as well as GO alone. The sharp and broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 11° (corresponding to d spacing = 8.01 Å) signifies that the conjugation process did not impede the crystalline structure of graphitic derivatives. However, after conjugation, an interlayer spacing of 4.2 (Å) was observed at 2θ = 21°. The XRD spectra of the conjugate demonstrates increase in the intensity of diffraction peaks which can be attributed to the augmentation in the d-spacing, mainly due to incorporation of peptides via cross-linkers, which led to partial exfoliation of the GO layers.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analysis was instrumental in confirming synthesis of the GO-DP1 nanoformulation (Fig. 2 b). Graphite displayed IR peaks at 3442 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 attributed to the presence of O-H and C=C bonds, respectively [51]. GO spectra illustrated the occurrence of prominent absorption peaks at 3437 cm−1, 1638 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 3658 cm−1. The strong and broad peak at 3437 cm−1 depicted O-H stretching, while the peaks at 1638 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 represent C=C stretching and C-H bending, respectively. Further, the sharp peak at 3658 cm−1 may be attributed to presence of the free OH groups [52]. The FT-IR spectra of peptide (DP1) displayed a characteristic peak at 3285 cm−1 which represents hydrogen bonded N-H stretching [53]. The spectra of GO-DP1 displayed adsorption peaks at 3381 cm−1, 1631 cm−1, 1382 cm−1 and 1276–1083 cm−1 that are remnants of the GO spectra. The broad and intense absorption peak at 3381 cm−1 represents amino groups overlapped by O-H stretching vibration [54]. Sharp IR peak at 1631 cm−1 along with another peak at 1382 cm−1 symbolizes C=O stretching of tertiary amide and C-H bending, respectively. Further, peak in the range 1276–1083 cm−1 signify occurrence of C-H and N-H deformation vibrations [55,56]. Upon comparing the spectra of GO-DP1 with DP1 and GO it was observed that the IR peaks became prominent after interaction with the peptide, thereby asserting conjugation between the two materials.

Elemental analysis of graphite, GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 was done using Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) equipped energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). The technique enables quantification of elements present in a specimen by visualizing the distribution of individual elements in a sample by two-dimensionally demonstrating the representative X-ray intensities or the compositional precision of the elements. During the synthesis of nanoconjugates, it was useful in conducting qualitative analysis of synthesized materials. Fig. 2(c-d) depicts elemental mapping and elemental weight percentage (%) at different sample positions. EDS spectra elucidates the presence of carbon and oxygen in graphite. Upon synthesis of GO, oxidation reaction results in decreased carbon and increased oxygen content. Siburian and co-workers have also reported a similar trend [57]. The elements carbon, oxygen, chlorine, sodium and nitrogen were observed in the EDS spectra of DP1. As expected, elemental mapping of GO-DP1 demonstrated presence of carbon and oxygen as a representation of graphitic composition along with nitrogen, chlorine and sodium that confirmed immobilization of the peptide (Fig. S3). It was interesting to note that oxygen percentage of the synthesized nanoformulation showed no significant difference. This pattern is accredited to amidation reaction between the N-terminal amide group of the peptides with carboxyl groups of GO [58,59].

The particle size, morphology and nanostructure of the formulated conjugate was determined by comparing micrographs of graphite and GO with GO-DP1 obtained by conducting FESEM (Fig. S2) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (Fig. 2e). Images of commercially procured pristine graphite presented in Fig. 2e (i) illustrates the presence of crumpled and closely stacked flake-like structure having multiple layers. GO was synthesized by application of sonication along with chemical oxidation procedure that leads to an exfoliated sheet-like structure having folds [60]. Presence of oxygen moieties on the surface and edges is responsible for the formation of fractures and wrinkles on the GO morphology, which in turn provides an increased surface for peptide attachment [61].

HR-TEM images of GO-DP1 demonstrates formation of spherical hybrids (Fig. 3 a iii). The particle size of the GO-DP1 hybrids was found to be 32 nm in diameter. Through Eq. (1), it was inferred that 24 peptide molecules were bound to GO after covalent functionalization. In addition, the spherical hybrids seemed well dispersed and did not show agglomeration which can be attributed to size control capabilities of the peptide [62]. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of graphite, GO and GO-DP1 were obtained in the range of 0–5.8 V, 2.1–5.1 V and 0–1.5 V, respectively (Fig. 3 b). The topology of graphite consisted of large globular clusters attributed to the aggregation of multi-layered sheets [63]. A similar topology was observed in HR-TEM micrographs as well (Fig. 3a). GO micrographs depicted the presence of a smooth flake-like topology having sharp edges. The sheets seemed to be closely packed with presence of wrinkles on the surface. The existence of covalently bound oxygen moieties along with displacement of hybridized carbon atoms during oxidation reaction, majorly accounts for increased thickness [64]. The GO-DP1 micrograph revealed a change in height with respect to GO alone that can be ascribed to the adhered peptide on GO surface, thereby resulting in a 2.5 µm thick GO-DP1 nanoformulation.

The biomedical applicability of a nanoconjugate is influenced by its electrokinetic potential as it is an indicator of its physical stability and tendency to form aggregates upon contact with biological media such as red blood cells (RBCs) and plasma [65]. Also, according to previous studies, peptide-based nanoconjugates with a slight negative or neutral charge are usually preferred for applications requiring in vivo administration [66]. The stability and dispersion behaviour of GO-DP1 with respect to surface charge, was determined by zeta potential measurements in an aqueous medium. Graphite and GO exhibited a negative zeta potential of −32.33 mV and −30 mV, respectively (Fig. 3c). Negative zeta potential values of GO are ascribed to electronegative functional moieties developed at the graphitic lattice during the course of oxidation reaction [67,68]. Moreover, during synthesis of GO, surge in the degree of oxidation takes place which results in an increase in the number of electronegative functional moieties on GO thereby increasing the zeta potential [67]. Since peptide has a positive surface charge, an increase in charge (−1.97 mV) was obvious upon covalent binding indicating neutralization of the negative charge of GO thereby once again confirming the functionalization of GO with DP1.

A nanoformulation tends to come in contact with liquids such as water, buffers, media and biological fluids, therefore it is important to conduct wetting studies as they provide insight into its hydrophobic or hydrophilic properties. To explore the wettability characteristics of GO-DP1, water contact angle (WCA) of graphite, GO and GO-DP1 was examined (Fig. 3d). The wetting property depends on factors such as chemical composition, morphology, high surface energy, presence of additional ionizable groups, pH and particle size [69,70]. Graphite was observed to be hydrophobic in nature (140°) and on synthesis of GO from graphite, it exhibited WCA of 25°. DP1 is an intrinsically hydrophilic peptide [13]. Upon conjugation of DP1 with GO, the WCA of nanoformulation decreased from 25° to 17°. The hydrophilicity exhibited by GO-DP1 may be attributed to the abundance of oxygen-containing moieties, primarily epoxides and hydroxyl groups on the basal plane of the nanoformulation, which has also been confirmed by the spectroscopic characterizations. Also, upon conjugation changes occur in the degree of ionization of groups, as a result, at low pH the functionalities present on GO undergo protonation, resulting in a decrease in the surface charge density thereby causing enhanced hydrophilicity amongst the synthesized nanoformulation [71]. As observed from the contact angle values it may be stated that the addition of amphipathic peptide (DP1) via cross-linkers conferred enhanced hydrophilicity to the formulated conjugates [34].

The antibacterial efficiency of DP1, GO and GO-DP1 was assessed against different Gram-positive (S. aureus, B. subtilis), Gram-negative (S. typhi, E. coli) and multidrug-resistant (MDR) organisms such as P. aeruginosa and strains of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). The latter MDR strains were particularly selected for evaluation of antibacterial activity because of the wide-spectrum hospital-acquired infections associated with them [72]. Also, these organisms cause contamination of surgical sites, bloodstream as well as urinary tract infections induced by catheter administration and ventilator-associated lung infections [63,73–76].

The antibacterial effect of the peptide-functionalized nanoformulation was assessed by conducting minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) studies along with the determination of zone of inhibition. DP1 inhibited growth of Gram-positive, Gram-negative and MDR bacteria in the range of 16 µg/mL–64 µg/mL [13,15,38,77]. MIC of GO was observed in the range of 256 µg/mL–512 µg/mL. GO-DP1 inhibited the growth of Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms in the range of 16 µg/mL–128 µg/mL. Growth of tested MDR organisms P. aeruginosa and MRSA was inhibited at a concentration in the range of 128 µg/mL–256 µg/mL. Nevertheless, MIC of the nanoformulation was observed in the active range of broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents. Overall, 2–8 fold change was observed in the antibacterial activity of GO-DP1 in comparison to GO. To the best, GO-PC’s MIC was reduced 8-fold against S. aureus.

Qualitative evaluation to access the antibacterial efficacy of DP1, GO and GO-DP1 was also conducted by the Kirby-Bauer well diffusion assay. It is a method predominantly used in industry to estimate the ability of novel materials to inhibit the growth of microorganisms. This technique is of particular importance while evaluating antibacterial activity of surface coatings, antibacterial textiles, fabrics and packaging materials as it provides comparable inhibitory activity levels in real-time applications [78,79]. Fig. 4 (b) displays the zone of inhibition obtained against different Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains. The inhibited zones were measured to determine the antibacterial effect of GO-DP1 in comparison to GO alone. It was observed that GO-DP1 formed zones of 14.7 mm, 22.2 mm, 12.26 mm, 12.2 mm, 24.1 mm, 25.2 mm, 13.7 mm and 15.8 mm against S. typhi, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, B. subtilis, MRSA 43300 and MRSA 13883, respectively. In comparison to GO, GO-DP1 displayed a minimum 2 mm increase in zone of inhibition diameter against all the tested bacterium. According to literature, a conjugate is said to exhibit synergistic antibacterial activity if its zone of inhibition displays an increased diameter by at least 2 mm in comparison to the control [80,81]. Thus, it is anticipated that GO-DP1 may constitute a new class of antibacterial agent having broad-spectrum biomedical applications.

In the synthesized nanoformulation, GO functions as the carrier of DP1 that aids the initial approach of the peptide in large concentration to the bacterial cell membrane as it provides a large surface area for peptide binding and functions as the carrier of DP1 that increases the bioavailability of the peptide and aids the initial approach of the peptide in large concentration to the bacterial cell membrane [23]. Since the peptide binds to the graphitic sheets either on the edges or the plane, the initial step of antibacterial mechanism is directed towards an electrostatic interaction that takes place between the cationic amino acid side chain of the peptide and the negatively charged bacterial membrane [82]. Along with this, the edges of the graphitic derivatives that act as “nano-knives” also come into action and make indentations on the bacterial membrane (Fig. 5 ). These primary interactions may play an important role in the initial rupture of the cell membrane that in turn facilitates the AMPs to move through the peptidoglycan layer and bind to the external lining of the cytoplasmic membrane [83]. It is deliberated that the disrupted outer membrane produces pores that further allow passage of the peptide-functionalized nanoformulation. These membrane openings also result in seepage of bacterial cell organelles that in turn causes cell death [84,85]. In a recent study conducted by our laboratory, the antibacterial mechanism of peptide DP1 on S. aureus was evaluated by dielectric spectroscopy and FESEM. It was found that the membrane conductivity of S. aureus cells was significantly lower when the bacterial cells were treated with DP1 [77]. These results were corroborated by FESEM micrographs which showed membrane disruption upon incubation of DP1 with bacterial cells [13,77,86]. Therefore, it is inferred that GO-DP1 also acts in a similar manner.

Small size of nanoconjugates enables rapid circulation through the body due to which evaluation of in vitro hemocompatibility is inevitable for newly synthesized antimicrobial agents [87]. The hemolytic toxicity of pristine graphite, GO and GO-DP1 was analysed by incubating human erythrocytes at concentrations ranging from 2 to 512 µg/mL (Fig. 6 a–c). At lower concentrations i.e., up to 32 µg/mL all the three materials DP1, GO and GO-DP1 were non-hemolytic. As the concentration increases beyond 32 µg/mL, DP1 showed a steep increase in hemolysis with 70 % survival at 64 µg/mL and only 40 % survival at 256 µg/mL which was further reduced to 30 % survival at 512 µg/ml. On the other hand, GO-DP1 even at concentrations above 256 µg/ml showed less than 10 % hemolysis and is considered non-hemolytic. Hence, GO-DP1 seems a prospective non-hemolytic and broad-spectrum antibacterial material for biomedical applications [88–90]. These results also ascertain that the coupling of the two materials was highly advantageous as it combats the hemolytic characteristic of DP1 while retaining the antimicrobial properties to a significant extent. In addition to this, viability studies to establish the effect of GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 on healthy mammalian cells 3T3-L1 was conducted (Fig. 6d). All the tested specimen displayed cell viability above 96 %, thereby exhibiting no cytotoxicity towards healthy cells.

Since GO-DP1 exhibited promising antibacterial activity, it was further evaluated for its effect on biofilm formation against P. aeruginosa which is a model bacterium for conducting biofilm related studies [91]. P. aeruginosa biofilm inhibition was assessed quantitatively and qualitatively in the presence of GO, DP1, and GO-DP1 at sub-minimum inhibitory concentrations (Fig. 7 a). In the absence of test specimen, P. aeruginosa was observed to produce a strong biofilm. However, GO-DP1 led to approximately 60 % reduction in mature P. aeruginosa biofilm. These results were in conformation to the crystal violet staining analysis during which decrease in cell density was evidently visible on the corresponding polystyrene walls of the microcentrifuge tubes (Fig. 7b).

During the qualitative biofilm inhibition assay, after undergoing incubation for a period of 7 days at 37 °C, the unbound bacteria was removed by washing. The remaining adhered biomass was stained with crystal violet dye, which revealed presence of violet coloured staining in the P. aeruginosa biofilm attributing to presence of matured biofilm having very strong adherence with the surface. GO treated Pseudomonas biofilm showed strong adherence but comparatively less than that of control biofilm. The reduction in biofilm biomass leading to detachment of cells from substrate walls upon incubation with GO may be due to its ability to permeate the polymeric matrix of Pseudomonas biofilm thereby reducing its three-dimensional assembly [82]. DP1 incubated biofilm showed moderate biomass adherence on the substrate walls, thereby signifying effectiveness in eradicating the established formation of Pseudomonas biofilm. Further, weak adherence of biomass was observed in the P. aeruginosa biofilm incubated with GO-DP1 nanoformulation signifying the presence of antibiofilm activity. It may be stated that GO exhibited biofilm adherence. However, GO-DP1 prevented colonization as well as elimination of biofilm from the substrate walls.

Effect of DP1, GO and GO-DP1 on the morphology of P. aeruginosa biofilm was observed through FESEM micrographs. Fig. 7(c-j) displays comparative FESEM micrographs of untreated P. aeruginosa biofilm at peak day (c-d), along with effect of GO (e-f), DP1 (g-h) and GO-DP1 (i-j) displaying their impact on biofilm inhibition. The micrographs of P. aeruginosa biofilm displayed a structured community of dense cells which seemed to be firmly embedded in extracellular polymeric substances. GO incubated P. aeruginosa biofilm displayed presence of loosely distributed cells with a decrease in biofilm. This suggested anti-adhesion property of GO. A substantial reduction in the biofilm structure along with the release of extracellular polymeric substance was observed after incubation with DP1 as well as GO-DP1. At higher magnification, presence of dented P. aeruginosa cells was observed which suggested that the nanoformulation inhibited P. aeruginosa biofilm by application of antibacterial as well as anti-adhesion activity. From these results it may be inferred that GO-DP1 nanoformulation has biofilm preventive as well as inhibitory properties.

P. aeruginosa is a multifaceted bacterium that is responsible for causing nosocomial infections in immunocompromised individuals. Infection management of P. aeruginosa is challenging mainly because it is naturally resistant to an array of antimicrobials. Recent studies have reported the prevalence of P. aeruginosa in healthcare settings. Surfaces of medical equipment and devices such as catheter, endoscope, arthroscope, ventilators, biopsy and hypodermic needle, are key transmission pathways and may operate as a reservoir for certain pathogens.

The clinical applicability of GO-DP1 was evaluated by incubating it with a P. aeruginosa contaminated hypodermic needle. Fig. 8 displays the experimental design which comprised incubation of P. aeruginosa infected needle under different experimental conditions for a duration of 24 h. In order to assess the effectiveness of the tested specimen towards inhibition of bacterial colonization and biofilm adhesion, the infected needles were incubated with GO, DP1 and GO-DP1 for a duration of 24 h. Following incubation biofilm formation was quantified by staining the incubated hypodermic needles with crystal violet dye. It was observed that DP1 and GO-DP1 incubated needles did not display biofilm formation whereas the needle incubated with GO showed moderately stained biofilm. On the other hand, crystal violet stained biofilm was observed in the control group where no antimicrobial agent was added during incubation. Proliferation of bacteria was also observed by determining the colony forming units of each specimen. As displayed in Fig. 8(c), GO-DP1 incubated group showed low bacterial proliferation. These findings are consistent with the quantitative as well as microscopic analysis conducted for determination of P. aeruginosa biofilm inhibition pattern (Fig. 7). Based on these findings, it is concluded that GO-DP1 has antibacterial and antibiofilm capability to combat the menace caused by pathogenic organisms in clinical settings.

In this study, a biocompatible nanoformulation of GO-DP1 with broad-spectrum antibacterial properties against Gram-positive, Gram-negative and multidrug-resistant bacterial strains has been developed. The nanoconjugates were approximately 32 nm in size with 24 DP1 molecules bound to a single GO sheet. DP1 alone has shown broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties but is hemolytic, on the other hand, GO is hemocompatible but shows negligible antibacterial activity. It was interesting to note that DP1 and GO complimented each other as the former imparts its antibacterial property to the conjugate and the latter contributes by lowering the hemolytic toxicity. This synergy between the two materials led to the development of a one-of-a-kind non-hemolytic, antibacterial nanoformulation that showed bacterial inhibition at MIC ranging from 16 to 256 µg/mL, 80 % hemocompatibility and successfully inhibited the growth of multidrug-resistant P. aeruginosa when used as a coating in hypodermic needles. In addition to this, 96 % cell viability affirmed cytocompatibility towards healthy cells. These results ascertain that covalently coupled GO-DP1 is a highly promising system to combat bacterial infections that is destined to find utility as surface coatings, antibacterial and antifouling additives along with therapeutic applications.
